item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article cannot be sourced, and thus it is to be assumed that it is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno if the author of this is still around to see this, or if they had any idea what they wrote. I am a Marine and a Cop and this scp deeply affected me. Maybe because In a certain sense I am the operators. I am that type of person, so dedicated to doing their job that they were willing to give up everything to keep doing it forever. Maybe because I have experienced the hightened state of awareness of being on mission, and thus I comprehend the operators chilling reality of being forever locked into that state in a way only people like me can. Maybe it affected me because In real life, perhaps not to this extreme, people like me stand at a similar crossroad and make a decision to become what we are. It involves some changes that can’t be undone. its a sacrifice where we lose some parts of our personality forever in order to become a tool to do things we believe make the world safer. But we’ll never get to enjoy that world because once you truly enter this one you can never go back. Theres a reason why cops and combat troops have such a high suicide rate after they leave the service. This scp draws out all those thoughts and feelings that I normally keep tucked away, and it makes me feel things where normally my emotions were long since cauterized.

I dunno if the author had a similar experience that inspired the scp, or if it was accidental. I don’t even know if others of my kind would be affected the same, by nature most are not as contemplative as me. I dunno if those without similar experience feel any impact from reading this scp, but if you do, remember that in the real world there are many “operators” in various professions who have made incredible sacrifices and work in the dark to serve the light. Remember them and enjoy the world they maybe cannot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thoroughly enjoyed it, and definitely feels appropriate within the Foundation lore.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. So we have a direct reference to the Daeva in this skip. Given that SCP-140 is the article on the site for explaining what this group is all about, a crosslink there would not go amiss. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2904 was recovered in 1935 from a dig site in Jammu and Kashmir, approximately ██ km north of Skardu. This dig site was excavated after information was retrieved from SCP-1726 which indicated that this site was of significance to the Daeva. Unlike other, similar sites that have been excavated, this site contained traces of non-Daeva occupation as well as the expected Daeva artefacts.

To this:

SCP-2904 was recovered in 1935 from a dig site in Jammu and Kashmir, approximately ██ km north of Skardu. This dig site was excavated after information was retrieved from SCP-1726 which indicated that this site was of significance to the Daeva. Unlike other, similar sites that have been excavated, this site contained traces of non-Daeva occupation as well as the expected Daeva artifacts.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon:

SCP-076-2 was exposed to this, or a Daeva adaptation of this. That's why he's stuck in Doomguy mode: his last memories of his brother and his home are of the betrayal; he doesn't remember forgiving his brother or going turncoat on the Daeva. He probably had a tattoo or something that has since been erased that said "Brother is forgiven. Kill the King."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful article. Great job writing an interesting story with actual progression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this tagged with "planet"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my goodness. This is quite stellar. It's got everything.

Interesting, new concept.
Provocative writing.
No small amount of fridge horror.
It's got that, I dunno. The 'feel' that this is the Foundation.
Everybody is characterized well.

I look forward to more of your work.

.Whenever anyone talks about applying amnesiacs to combat memetic effects, I think I'll link them here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got me there; it is a weakness. A minor one though, what are the chances?

However; you'll note they use two factor authentication. As well as the music they use what are referred to as "the badges." These indicate to the Operator that "this is someone you can trust." The music just sets them at ease, helps them relax and makes violence less of an immediate response. As they are, the Operators are incapable of totally letting their guard down.

The dementia thing was deliberatly subtle. It's a sensitive subject and I wouldn't wan't to be a dick about it. Still, I may have been a little too subtle. My bad. Having it "click" later is exactly what I hoped would happen though. When I read and that happens I always find it very satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was absolutely fantastic! Thank you for writing such a compelling article.

Also, yes, "I Don't Like Mondays" is a fantastic song, and well-chosen here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really surprises me that you hadn't heard of HM: that's where my mind immediately jumped as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Science diversion!

+ show block
– hide block

H.M. was a huge, huge deal in neuroscience and psychology. (Here's a video that might be less dry than Wikipedia.) In the 1950's, he had significant portions of his brain removed to treat severe epilepsy, and developed both partial retrograde and extreme anterograde amnesia; he basically lived in a constant, few-minute present until he died.

Studying the sections of his brain that were missing as well as the functions of memory he was or was not capable of led to probably the majority of current understanding of memory and brain functions.

One of my professors met him once unexpectedly, and couldn't think of anything to ask but, "What's it like not to have any new memories?" To which H.M. said, "What!?" and was briefly freaked out until he forgot the question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow! This was a long read, but a very compelling one. The letter preceding the article was brilliant at setting up the object itself, although I didn't pick up on the writer suffering from dementia before exposure to the object when I first read it, the later addendum mentioning dementia/alzheimers made it click for me.

The only flaw I noticed is that simply having a song telling Operators that they're inactive could backfire terribly if hostile forces happened to be listening to that song when an Operator kicks down the door.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh,

This was two separate but connected pieces until the last minute. I felt that the first part, the letter, might be interesting on its own but it doesn't explain enough and would be unsatisfying. I also got some reliable advice that they're better together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, you're right.

Oblate spheroids need another measurement to be precisely described, but I didn't want to overload what was already a number-heavy paragraph. The exact shape isn't important to the article. I could have used the polar radius or a ratio I believe is called oblacity.

Didn't think I'd get picked up on that. Should have known better. Good spot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Operators are conditioned to hear their song and know that they are not operational and can relax, not kill anybody.

Just for reference, "I Don't Like Mondays" was originally about a school shooting.

Also, "The silicon chip inside her head gets switched to overload", huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it could be better if you put the document on separate page? That document deserves its own tales page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definite +1 from me.

I do have a question about your oblate spheroid. You've only given the equatorial radius for the object, whereas I would have expected another dimension as well. Is that normal for describing something that shape? I read the Wikipedia entry in case there was an obvious answer, but no joy.

Very minor nitpick I know, but an important one, especially for the scientists out there1

1. definitelynotascientist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was really cool and well thought out. You got my feels and my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

I hadn't heard of H.M. (thanks for bringing him to my attention, interesting!) but I was thinking of "weaponised dementia" later in the writing. These guys have a number of advantages over baseline humans but the Foundation finds a way to use their biggest weakness as a strength.

The before section was supposed to hint that the writer had memory issues, probably caused by the early stages of dementia. I may have been too subtle there. The pause before his secretary answers is because he's using the name of the person before her by mistake.

The not Daeva line was a very late addition. Before that I got some complaints about that bit and people just weren't getting it. Probably my fault. If I just dropped the "not Daeva" line and kept the "non-Daeva occupation" and "style distinctly different to that typical of Daeva artifacts" lines do you think it would work better?

I only found out about the D-class contest yesterday, just before I posted. I wrote this a while before that. Honestly, I don't think it fits the remit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, TL333s!

The note is supposed to be a way for the recruiters to dissuade potential Operators. It's not for everyone and they don't want to trap someone into that life without giving them fair warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spot on, Scorpian451.

The Operators go through an intensive preparation programme before exposure to the object and getting locked in.

There was a whole addendum about this but I cut it for being boring to read.

The Boomtown Rats song (which is amazing, if you haven't heard it) is part of the conditioning. The Operators are conditioned to hear their song and know that they are not operational and can relax, not kill anybody.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic. It has excellent pathos, and a really solid exploration of consequences. Also, reading the opening note, my immediate thought was, "Oh man, they've weaponized H.M.!", so from a science standpoint1, mission accomplished.

If there are any downsides, the "before" section of the opening note didn't make a ton of sense, and the "Not Daeva!" line was a little blunt, but I'm not pushing those. They both work well where they are.

My only other thought was, half-seriously, why not wait for the D-Class contest? This is right on the edge of a reimagining of the system…

Footnotes
1. coughcoughasascientistcoughcough

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They did prepare them beforehand, so the conditioning might be part of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really sure what the initial note is for, but this whole thing is well written and really compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, I don't understand something—

The introductory letter says that she's been conditioned to dictate her thoughts, but the Description says that 2904-1 instances can't learn by conditioning.

Also, what's the Boomtown Rats song for? Sorry, I'm dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was brilliant, heavy, and beautiful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not expect this to be so clever and sad when I began reading.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ability of Foundation writers to make me care about fictional characters continues to impress me.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
If you've got this far; thanks for reading!

I'm completely open to any criticism, although I may be slow to respond; life things.

EDIT: Being the ungrateful type that I am, I forgot to thank those who helped make this readable. Thanks go to shaggydredlocks and another in the IRC. Sorry for forgetting your name, another. I should really get a system. You guys made me start to believe in this piece. It wouldn't have happened without you. (Seriously: if you can remind me of the your name I'll give you top billing in the thanks.)

Thanks also to MrWrong and Quantum Physician in the forums. MrWrong was harsh, but fair and I needed some of that. Quantum Physician was nicer but didn't shy away from pointing out what needed pointing out. I needed some of that too.
